data.title: ECB unlikely to change rates in next meeting, Escriva says
data.description:
The European Central Bank is very unlikely to change rates at its next meeting and will make any decisions on a meeting-by-meeting basis, ECB policymaker Jose Luis Escriva said on Friday.

Escriva told regional Catalan TV3 television that the bank would need more time to assess the full impact of the war in the Middle East before making decisions.

"With the information I have, I think it's very unlikely that we will touch rates at the next meeting," Escriva said, adding that "we can already take it for granted that there will be effects" from the war.

But he also said these could be sharply limited if the war were to end soon: "The effects on what matters to consumers – everyday prices – we are talking about tenths of a percent, not much more."

The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, which has spread to other countries in the Gulf, is threatening to drive up inflation and hit sluggish euro zone growth by making energy more expensive and disrupting supply chains.

"Our inflation target of 2% is a medium-term horizon, transitory movements should not necessarily lead us to make decisions. Instead, we must monitor the situation and assess to what extent this is having more persistent effects over time," Escriva said.

The ECB left rates unchanged at its last meeting in February and signalled comfort in the outlook, but the situation has materially shifted this week with war and the surge in energy prices.

Investors now see some chances of an ECB rate hike by December.

Escriva, who is also the Bank of Spain's governor, said it was too early to assess the impact of any halt in trade between Spain and the U.S. following President Donald Trump's threats to cut commercial ties over Spain's stance on the conflict, since there was no firm decision yet.

data.title: Poland central bank sees CPI within target through 2028 in report that predates Iran war
data.description:
Poland's central bank said inflation is likely to remain within its target range until the end of 2028 in a report released Friday, but based on data that predates the start of the conflict in the Middle East.

The National Bank of Poland projected in its March forecasts that inflation will reach 2.3% this year, 2.4% in 2027 and 2.3% in 2028 - around the middle of the central bank's 1.5%-3.5% inflation target - based on data available until February 19.

Growth will slow slightly to 3.9% this year, from 4.0% in 2025, the report said.

The U.S. and Israel have bombarded Iran since the weekend, killing Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei in the opening salvos of the conflict. Iran has responded with strikes around the region and the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, which handles roughly one-fifth of the world's daily oil supply.

Crude oil is set for its sharpest weekly gain in four years, stoking fears of quicker inflation worldwide.

The war in the Middle East means there is less space for rate cuts in Poland, central bank official Ludwik Kotecki told TOK FM radio on Friday.

A day earlier, central bank governor Adam Glapinski said that the war poses the biggest risk for inflation in Poland, but if the situation calms down fairly quickly, the Polish Monetary Policy Council could cut interest rates further.

Swaps traders currently expect three quarter-point reductions in the key rate to 3% by year-end, according to LSEG data.

In Friday's report, the central bank predicted economic growth will be driven by continued robust domestic consumption.

An acceleration in investment is also expected over the three-year forecast horizon, driven by increased spending on energy transformation, the construction of railway infrastructure and a new airport, and military equipment purchases.

The expected peak absorption of European Union funds will also be a key driven this year, but "over the longer projection horizon, the reduced use of these funds will translate into a slowdown in investment and economic activity," the report said.

A factor that could significantly affect the path of energy prices and inflation in 2028 is the planned expansion of the EU’s CO₂ emissions trading system to new sectors, the report said, although it is still unclear what the price levels will be.

data.title: Rheinmetall: well-positioned to play key role as industrial partner in rockets
data.description:
German defence company Rheinmetall said on Friday that its operations in the Arab region underpin its efforts to expand its rocket manufacturing capacity in the Western world as quickly as possible.

"We see ourselves ideally positioned to play a key role as an industrial partner in rockets and components," said the company.

data.title: Blanket evacuation orders in Lebanon raise international law concerns, says UN human rights chief
data.description:
By Olivia Le Poidevin

Large-scale evacuation orders issued by the Israeli army for southern Lebanon and Beirut's southern suburbs raise serious concerns under international law, the U.N. human rights chief said on Friday.

"These blanket, massive displacement orders we are talking here about hundreds and thousands of people," said United Nations human rights chief, Volker Turk.

"This raises serious concern under international humanitarian law, and in particular when it comes to issues around forced transfer," he added.

Israel carried out heavy airstrikes on the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs of Beirut overnight after ordering its residents to leave, while the Iran-backed group warned Israelis to leave towns and villages at the frontier.

On Thursday, an Israeli military spokesperson told residents of the southern suburbs to move east and north, posting a map showing four large districts of the capital he said they must leave, including areas adjacent to Beirut's airport.

Lebanon was pulled into the war in the Middle East on Monday when Hezbollah opened fire, triggering a new Israeli offensive, with airstrikes focused on Beirut's southern suburbs and on southern and ⁠eastern Lebanon.

"Lebanon is becoming a key flashpoint. I'm extremely concerned and worried about the latest developments," Turk said in Geneva following the firing of rockets into Israel by Hezbollah and the Israeli army's strong response in recent days.

Hezbollah, in a message published in Hebrew on its Telegram channel early on Friday, warned Israelis to leave towns within 5 km (3 miles) of the border.

During fighting between Hezbollah and Israel in 2024, tens of thousands of Israelis were evacuated from towns in the border area, ‌but ⁠many have since returned. Israeli officials have previously said there are no plans to remove them for now.

"The world urgently needs to see steps to contain the Middle East crisis," Turk added.

data.title: DHL Group Has Near-Term Upside Earnings Potential From Middle East Conflict — Market Talk
data.description: DHL Group is focused on resuming profitable growth, while the Middle East conflict offersthe German logistics company near-term upside earnings potential, JPMorgan analysts write. The closure of major Middle East airports and the Strait of Hormuz, and the potential spillover effects from disruption to global air and sea capacity, may offer near-term upside for the company's express business, the bank says. "DHL's global diversified network is well-positioned to service freight through alternative routes." JPMorgan says DHL Express is the dominant player in the region so continued air and sea disruption will likely create some volume growth opportunity. The bank raises its price target on the stock to 55 euros from 53.50 euros and reiterates its overweight rating. Shares rise 2.6% to 47.08 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: European prices face more volatility amid growing LNG competition
data.description:
Dutch and British wholesale gas prices slowed huge gains seen this week in the wake of the Iran crisis on Friday morning, but remain volatile amid growing competition among buyers globally to replace lost Qatari liquefied natural gas (LNG) cargoes.

The benchmark Dutch front-month contract at the TTF hub (TFMBMc1) was up 1.02 euros at 51.75 euros per megawatt hour (MWh) by 1026 GMT, data from the Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) showed.

The British contract for April (NGLNMJ6) was up 2.25 pence at 133.75 pence per therm, after initially trading 6.00 pence lower.

European gas prices have surged this week as the Iran conflict has shutdown exports of LNG from Qatar, which provides some 20% of global supply.

The majority of Qatari LNG is sold to Asia, where buyers are now seeking alternatives and several LNG tankers from the US as well as Nigeria are already diverting to Asia and away from Europe.

Qatar's energy minister Saad al-Kaabi said that even if the war ended immediately it would take Qatar "weeks to months" to return to a normal cycle of deliveries, he told the Financial Times in an interview published on Friday.

Based on current prices, Europe is losing the bidding war with Asian competitors over US cargoes, analysts at Bernstein said in a note.

"TTF will likely need to jump another 40% to 50% from where it is currently, just to keep the lights on, if the Qatari outage persists beyond a week or two," they added.

Meanwhile, Italy, which sources 25% of its LNG from Qatar and where utility Enel said it has lost five April cargoes, said on Friday it is looking for alternative suppliers.

Still, Europe is in a much better position in the short-term and can afford some cargo diversions, said LSEG analyst Saku Jussila.

"However, this is not sustainable through all summer if the conflict gets prolonged," he added.

LSEG lowered its European LNG send-out forecast for April by around 400 gigawatt hours (GWh) per day because of the conflict and lowered its 46-day storage outlook, Jussila said.

EU gas storage sites were last 29.6% full, compared with around 37.2% at the same time last year, Gas Infrastructure Europe data showed.

In the European carbon market, the benchmark contract (CFI2Zc1) inched up by 0.62 euro to 70.97 euros a metric ton.

data.title: U.S. Stagflation Risk Raises Concern Ahead of Jobs Data — Market Talk
data.description: Investors are concerned about the risk of stagnant growth and rising inflation in the U.S. ahead of non-farm payrolls data due at 1330 GMT, Tickmill Group's Patrick Munnelly says in a note. Rising energy costs due to the Middle East conflict have reignited fears about inflation and caused markets to lower expectations of the U.S. Federal Reserve cutting interest rates in the coming months. Investors will pay attention to the U.S. jobs data as strong data could indicate that "the economy can still withstand higher-for-longer rates", Munnelly says. A weak jobs report could heighten fears about economic weakness, however, he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: HOOD Stock Gains Pre-Market After Cathie Wood’s ARK Invest Scoops Up $15M Stake
data.description:
Key points:
  • Cathie Wood’s ARK Invest purchased nearly $15 million worth of Robinhood shares across three ETFs during Wednesday’s market decline.
  • The largest share went to the asset manager’s flagship ARK Innovation ETF, with smaller allocations to ARK Fintech Innovation ETF and ARK Next Generation Internet ETF.
  • Retail sentiment on Stockwits across the three funds varied on Friday morning.

Cathie Wood’s ARK Invest bought up nearly $15 million worth of Robinhood (HOOD) shares as the market dropped on Wednesday.

Wood’s purchase comes after CEO Vlad Tenev launched Robinhood’s new “invite-only” platinum credit card during the company’s ‘Take Flight’ keynote event. She added 158,259 Robinhood shares to the ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK), another 5,267 shares to the ARK Fintech Innovation ETF (ARKF), and 20,407 shares to the ARK Next Generation Internet ETF (ARKW). 

As per HOOD’s closing price of $80.56, after the stock fell more than 2%, the purchase cost Wood around $14.8 million. HOOD’s stock edged 0.45% in pre-market trade on Friday, with retail sentiment around the company trending in ‘bearish’ territory on Stocktwits. Chatter rose to ‘normal’ from ‘low’ levels over the past day. 

Retail Sentiment Across ARK Funds Shows Mixed Signals

Retail sentiment on Stocktwits across ARKK, ARKF, and ARKW varied on Friday. The asset manager’s flagship ARK Innovation ETF edged 0.42% higher in pre-market trade, with retail sentiment trending in ‘bullish’ territory over the past day and chatter at ‘normal’ levels. 

Meanwhile, the ARK Fintech Innovation ETF moved 0.6% higher in pre-market trade on Friday, but retail sentiment trended in ‘bearish’ territory over the past day. Chatter remained at ‘high’ levels.

Retail sentiment around the ARK Next Generation Internet ETF fell to ‘neutral’ from ‘bullish’ territory over the past day. It also saw chatter trending at ‘high’ levels.

One user said that ARK is scooping up more Robinhood shares in anticipation that HOOD’s stock will break its record high.

https://www.stocktwits.com/sister/message/646624613

Announcements From Robinhood’s ‘Take Flight’ Keynote Event

Robinhood’s new $695-a-year card offers rewards like 5% back on flights, 10% back on hotels, and 5% cash back on dining, among other perks. The company also announced the launch of a “family hub” where all accounts from a single household will be visible in one place. Robinhood said the feature will likely roll out later this year. 

It also announced custodial accounts, designed to allow parents and guardians to invest on behalf of minors, as well as trust accounts for customers using revocable living trusts as part of estate planning and wealth transfer strategies.

data.title: Rheinmetall Could Be Hit By Purchasing Shift Toward Drones — Market Talk
data.description: Rheinmetall's growth forecasts could be hit by several emerging trends, including a shift in NATO's purchasing priorities toward drones, Mwb-research analyst Jens-Peter Rieck says. The analyst also highlights increasing interest in artificial-intelligence-enabled systems and networked warfare--which combines data from several sources to take quick battlefield decisions--, and away from conventional heavy land equipment, the main earnings driver for the German company. Competition among European defense companies is intensifying, particularly in areas such as drone systems and integrated battlefield software, he notes. Mwb-research downgrades its Rheinmetall rating to hold from buy, and its target a price range between 1,700 euros and 2,000 euros. Shares are up 2.7% at 1,589 euros. (cristina.gallardo@wsj.com)

data.title: Dollar Could Rise if U.S. Jobs Data Dampen Rate Cut Bets Further — Market Talk
data.description: The dollar could rise if upcoming U.S. jobs data are solid enough to encourage a further reduction in Federal Reserve interest-rate cut expectations, MUFG Bank's Derek Halpenny says in a note. The nonfarm payrolls report will be released at 1330 GMT. Given concerns about higher inflation as energy prices rise on the Middle East conflict, a stronger payrolls print and an acceleration in wage growth would potentially prompt a further scaling back of rate-cut bets and lift the dollar, he says. However, if the conflict drags on longer, Friday's jobs data will "probably be the best we see for a while" given the potential impact on businesses. The DXY dollar index falls 0.1% to 99.247. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

data.title: Aperol Maker Bets More Americans Will Warm to Italian-Style Drinking — Interview
data.description:
By Joshua Kirby

The maker of Aperol is banking on selling a taste of Italy's terrace lifestyle to more Americans, part of its chief executive's plan to grab market share and sales growth in the U.S.

"Half of America has still never heard of [Aperol]," Davide Campari-Milano Chief Executive Simon Hunt said in an interview.

That gives the Italian distiller a big job to do stateside, Hunt said.

Working in Campari's favor is the lure of the Mediterranean dolce vita.

"Sitting outside in the sun on a beautiful piazza with your friends and food is hugely aspirational for the rest of the world," Hunt said.

Campari has significantly increased advertising and promotional spending in the U.S., he said. A&P is a key means for liquor firms to seduce new drinkers and boost sales.

Sales of Aperol are already on the rise in the U.S. as American drinkers acquire a taste for the aperitif, commonly mixed with sparkling wine and soda to make an Aperol Spritz. Aperol makes up over a quarter of Campari's sales, and the group is doubling down on the drink.

"We have seen double-digit growth [in Aperol sales] in many of the markets we've entered in the last couple of years," Hunt said.

The group, whose brands also include its namesake Campari bitters and cognac house Courvoisier, as well as Wild Turkey bourbon and Espolon tequila, spent close to 18% of sales on A&P spending in 2025 and plans to increase that further in 2026, it said in an earnings update this week. Aperol will take the largest share of that spending.

The group is counting on investment to arrest a slide in alcohol sales in many markets, including the U.S., where people are drinking less against a chilly consumer backdrop. Booze makers are also contending with growing interest in legal marijuana as an alternative to drinking, and with sobriety for health reasons. Total off-premise alcohol sales--drinks bought in stores rather than bars--fell 3.4% last year in the U.S., according to a report compiled by consumer-research firm NIQ.

"We are cautious on the outlook for the U.S., where the industry remains in decline," analysts at UBS wrote in a note following Campari's update. Like many of its peers, Campari has suffered a crash in its share price in recent years as investors, spooked by downward trends in volumes, cool on the industry. Milan-listed shares closed Thursday at 6.54 euros, down from a postpandemic high of 12.69 euros in June 2023.

Hunt, who took the helm at Campari in January 2025, is betting on a streamlined strategy that places less interest in adding labels to the group portfolio and adds greater focus on core brands like Aperol.

Still, Campari risks being challenged by rivals in the U.S., where it remains a minnow in terms of market share. London-based drinks group Diageo, maker of Smirnoff vodka and Johnnie Walker whisky, is eyeing a greater share of the mass-market segment, threatening Campari's own brands.

But Hunt said he wouldn't be drawn into a price war.

"Anyone can drop prices," he said. "That's not the game we're playing. We believe a longer-term equity play is more sustainable."

Campari's bet on promotional spending contrasts with the tack taken by some peers. Diageo is planning to spend around 10% less on A&P in 2026 than last year, promising a "more effective and efficient" approach to outlay, boss Dave Lewis told analysts. And French group Pernod Ricard, maker of Absolut vodka and Martell cognac, said it plans to keep ad spending unchanged as a proportion of sales, despite a slide in its top line.

"We're clear about where we want to win and what investment is needed," Hunt said.

Write to Joshua Kirby at joshua.kirby@wsj.com

data.title: Maersk, Hapag-Lloyd Suspend Key Middle East Shipping Routes — WSJ
data.description:
By Dominic Chopping

Container shipping groups A.P. Moller-Maersk and Hapag-Lloyd suspended key shipping routes into and out of the Middle East as the fallout from the war in the region continues to disrupt trade flows.

Maersk said Friday that it suspended shipping routes connecting the Middle East with Europe and the Far East as well as its Gulf shuttle services. German shipping peer Hapag-Lloyd also said it had suspended several services. Shares in both companies were slightly lower.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).
